5 min read
Just now

--

Photo by Kevin Canlas on UnsplashIntroduction
Here, I am going to write the pseudocode for the logical analysis that I had written in my last post of part 2. So, yes, we are going to discuss how to write pseudocode and later in my next post, I will be translating this pseudocode into the actual code syntax.
Now, you may be wondering what is pseudocode? You may have read lots of articles in the net with rigorous rules to write pseudocode, but, relax! No need to fret! Every one does not need to follow all the rules of standard pseudocode, just follow the minimum standard rules,that’s all!
For instance, pseudocode should be written in plain English and not in code! A pseudocode is not the same as Algorithm! Algorithm is a process of breaking down of complex problem into simpler shorter bits or steps in plain English and pseudocode after writing Algorithm involves writing down these simple steps in a particular sequence for solving the problem, which is easier to translate to code syntax. The main rule you need to follow is that it is in plain English and in a finite number of specific sequence and need to be written line by line with one instruction in every line separately and main task will have sub tasks under it and they will be indented to show the separation accordingly. A pseudocode is similar to code but not exactly into coding.The main keywords for doing the coding later will be written in pseudocode in capital words, to highlight the important steps in the program.
Writing Pseudocode for the Todo List Program
STARTCREATE an empty list called toDoList
LOOP FOREVER:
DISPLAY “ToDo List Manager”
INPUT menu_choice from user
IF menu_choice is “view”:
FOR EACH item in toDoList:
DISPLAY item
ELSE IF menu_choice is “add”:
INPUT new_item from user
ADD new_item to toDoList
ELSE IF menu_choice is “remove”:
INPUT item_to_remove from user
INPUT confirmation from user
IF confirmation is “yes”:
REMOVE item_to_remove from toDoList
ELSE IF menu_choice is “edit”:
INPUT item_to_edit from user
INPUT replacement_item from user
REPLACE item_to_edit WITH replacement_item in toDoList
ELSE IF menu_choice is “delete”:
CLEAR all items from toDoList
ELSE IF menu_choice is “exit”:
BREAK out of the loop
WAIT 1 second
CLEAR the screen
END
Explanation
In the above pseudocode, the keyword, START as written in capital word is to indicate the phase where the Program starts processing.
The keyword CREATE is another keyword to show emphasis on the beginning phase of the program where we start the program by creating an empty list to store the todo items.
The LOOP FOREVER keyword is a continuous chain or loop that we need in the program to have an infinite process of the program accepting data from the user, storing it, processing it, displaying it, editing it, removing it or clearing the entire list if requested, until the user terminates the program. Since we are not yet into the coding syntaxes, but into pseudocode, we use the English keyword LOOP FOREVER to signify the infinite loop.
Please note that we are following the Python-like indentations in writing the pseudocode so that when we translate this later into our python script, it is so much more easier for us!
Inside the infinite loop, we are showing the actions pertaining to DISPLAY of the menu choices to the user in order to select various actions, if to view items, add an item, delete an item, edit an item, etc. The user will decide what to INPUT to the program based on his/her choice.
We have used the IF ELSE conditional statements that we also use in Python, the only difference here is that we have mentioned that in capital words, so we know, it is not a syntax but, it will do the job of thinking about the program process in an easy functaional manner! This condition will be there throughout the program to help decision making for the user and program and for the user to interact with the program. For example, if the user’s option is to view the items added to the list, then the program will loop through each and every item in the list ( suggested by the pseudocode as FOR EACH)and DISPLAY those items to the user.
ELSE IF the user selects the choice of adding a new item, the program will accept the INPUT from the user and then ADD this new item to the todo list that was created at the beginning of the program.
You can follow the same rule to write the pseudocode logic for the other processes in the program, that is, editing the existing item, removing any item if required, exiting the program and deleting the entire list. I want you to take a good look at every step of the pseudocode and then write it down yourself couple of times to get a hang of it!
A Quick Note
The keyword break is tantamount to an emergency exit door. Once the program hits the break, it will just ignore everything below it inside the loop (it won't even wait 1 second or clear the screen) and jumps straight to the end of the file. You will get to know this more clearly while I explain this process while I do the code syntax for this part in the next post! And why do we need to exit the program? Obviously we don't want a program to run infinitely, do we?
A small cheat sheet to take note of
Please note here as mentioned earlier, I am using the important keywords in Python in capital words in order for your muscle memory to identify and remember it later when you code the syntaxes in Python!
The Indentation:
Do you notice, how the indentation in the pseudocode matches the indentation we will use in Python? This helps in building the muscle memory for Python’s strict indentation specifications.
The “LOOP FOREVER” keyword:
In Python code, we use the while True loop for representing an infinite loop, but, many beginners are intimidated with this terminology, hence, representing it as LOOP FOREVER in the Pseudocode will ensure that we are reminded of the fact that the menu in the program will keep showing up until and unless, we shut the program manually!
Pseudocode vs Code:
Please note that Pseudocode is what we want to write in order to clarify how the program runs to prepare us for writing the code syntax later on. However, the code syntax in Python is the instructions that the computer will strictly understand and is the specific instructions that we want the computer to carry out.
Conclusion
In this post, I wrote about Pseudocode, how to write it, why write it and correlated it with the algorithmic thinking and actual code syntaxes and it’s significance before doing the actual coding.
In the next post, I will be writing the actual Python code for this project and really deep diving into each and every line of code snippet there!
